title: Anthropic Keeps Its Most Powerful Model Private After It Finds Vulnerabilities in Every Major OS and Browser
summary: Anthropic launched Project Glasswing, restricting access to its most capable model, Claude Mythos Preview, to ~50 defensive security partners after it autonomously discovered thousands of high-severity vulnerabilities across every major operating system and web browser.
content: Anthropic debuted Claude Mythos Preview — its most capable frontier model — as part of Project Glasswing, a new cybersecurity initiative involving partners such as Nvidia, Google, AWS, Apple, Microsoft, JPMorgan Chase, Broadcom, Cisco, CrowdStrike, the Linux Foundation, and Palo Alto Networks, plus roughly 40 additional organizations that build or maintain critical software infrastructure. Though Claude Mythos Preview was not specifically trained for cybersecurity, Anthropic says the model's strong agentic coding and reasoning skills enabled it to flag 'thousands of high-severity vulnerabilities, including some in every major operating system and web browser,' developing many related exploits 'entirely autonomously, without any human steering.' Anthropic stated it has no plans to make the model publicly available due to those same cybersecurity capabilities, committing instead up to $100 million in usage credits for Glasswing partners and $4 million in direct donations to the Linux Foundation and Apache Software Foundation. Newton Cheng, Anthropic's frontier red team cyber lead, said the goal is to give cyber defenders a 'head start' against adversaries, while the company privately briefed senior US government officials on Mythos Preview's full offensive and defensive capabilities. Anthropic plans to eventually release Mythos-class models publicly, but only after refining new safeguards through a forthcoming Claude Opus model first.

title: Meta Freezes AI Data Work After Supply Chain Breach Exposes AI Training Secrets
summary: Meta indefinitely suspended its collaboration with AI data startup Mercor after a supply chain attack via a poisoned open-source library exposed sensitive training methodologies that power the world's leading large language models.
content: Meta has paused its work with Mercor — a $10 billion San Francisco AI data startup that generates bespoke training datasets for major AI labs including Meta, OpenAI, Anthropic, and Google — after a cyberattack exposed sensitive information about how those companies train their models. The breach originated with threat group TeamPCP, which compromised the LiteLLM open-source Python library (97 million monthly downloads) and on March 27, 2026 published two malicious versions to PyPI that harvested API keys, cloud credentials, and CI/CD secrets before being removed roughly 40 minutes later. Mercor confirmed the breach exposed approximately four terabytes of data, including 939 gigabytes of platform source code, a 211-gigabyte user database, and roughly three terabytes of video interview recordings and identity documents belonging to more than 40,000 people. Most alarming to the AI industry is the potential exposure of data selection criteria, labeling protocols, and training strategies — competitive secrets that companies have spent billions developing and rarely discuss publicly. OpenAI is investigating but has not paused its work with Mercor; Anthropic and Google have not publicly commented on their exposure; and hacking group Lapsus$ has claimed responsibility and begun auctioning the stolen data on dark web forums, while a class action lawsuit was filed on April 1 on behalf of affected contractors.

title: Google Releases Gemma 4: Open AI Models That Outcompete Rivals 20x Their Size
summary: Google DeepMind launched Gemma 4, its most capable family of open-weight AI models, available under a commercially permissive Apache 2.0 license, with the flagship 31B model ranking third among all open models worldwide on the Arena AI leaderboard.
content: Google DeepMind released Gemma 4, a family of four open models — Effective 2B (E2B), Effective 4B (E4B), 26B Mixture of Experts, and 31B Dense — built on the same research and technology as Gemini 3 and freely available under a commercially permissive Apache 2.0 license. The flagship 31B model currently ranks #3 among open text models globally on the Arena AI leaderboard and the 26B MoE model ranks #6, with both outperforming competing models up to 20 times their size; the 26B MoE model achieves this by activating only 3.8 billion of its total parameters during inference, delivering exceptional speed. All four variants support multimodal inputs including text, images, and audio, with context windows up to 256K tokens, native agentic workflow support via function calling and structured JSON output, and compatibility with over 140 languages. The edge-optimized E2B and E4B models run completely offline with near-zero latency on mobile devices, Raspberry Pi, and NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano, bringing frontier-class AI to billions of Android devices. Since the original Gemma launch, the family has been downloaded more than 400 million times and the developer community has created over 100,000 model variants, with real-world applications spanning a pioneering Bulgarian-language model and Yale University research into cancer therapy pathways.

title: White House Releases National AI Legislative Framework, Calls for Federal Preemption of State Laws
summary: The Trump administration released a seven-pillar National AI Legislative Framework calling on Congress to establish a uniform federal AI standard that would preempt the growing patchwork of state AI regulations, immediately triggering competing legislation from both parties.
content: On March 20, 2026, the White House released its National AI Legislative Framework — a nonbinding but consequential legislative roadmap directing Congress to act on seven AI policy priorities: child protection, AI infrastructure, intellectual property, free speech, innovation, workforce development, and a national framework that would preempt state AI laws the administration considers unduly burdensome. The framework's most consequential provision calls on Congress to replace the existing patchwork of state AI regulations — advanced by California, New York, Colorado, and dozens of other jurisdictions — with a single minimally burdensome national standard, while preserving limited state authority over traditional police powers and zoning. Within five days, the proposal drew sharp partisan responses: Senator Marsha Blackburn (R-TN) introduced the TRUMP AMERICA AI Act to codify the framework's elements, while Senators Sanders and Representative Ocasio-Cortez introduced the AI Data Center Moratorium Act calling for a nationwide pause on data center construction until comprehensive federal safeguards are enacted. Democratic lawmakers have argued the framework prioritizes preemption without establishing sufficiently robust national safeguards, and a House bill (H.R. 8031) was introduced the same day to repeal the administration's preemption efforts entirely. The path forward in Congress remains highly uncertain, with competing proposals, jurisdictional complexity, and deep divisions within and across both parties over the scope and structure of federal AI regulation.

title: Survey: 79% of Enterprises Struggle to Adopt AI Despite Near-Universal Deployment
summary: A new survey by WRITER and Workplace Intelligence finds that while 97% of executives have deployed AI agents, 79% of organizations face significant adoption challenges — a double-digit jump from 2025 — with only 29% reporting meaningful ROI from generative AI.
content: WRITER's 2026 AI Adoption in the Enterprise survey, conducted with independent research firm Workplace Intelligence across 1,200 non-technical employees and 1,200 C-suite executives globally, reveals that AI adoption has shifted from technical tension to a deep cultural and organizational crisis. Nearly all executives (97%) report deploying AI agents in the past year, with 52% of employees already using them daily, yet 79% of organizations face significant adoption challenges — a double-digit increase from 2025 — and 54% of C-suite executives admit that adopting AI is 'tearing their company apart.' Three-quarters of executives (75%) admit their company's AI strategy is 'more for show' than actual internal guidance, 48% call AI adoption a 'massive disappointment,' and only 29% report significant ROI from generative AI despite AI super-users delivering 5X individual productivity gains. A dangerous two-tiered workplace is emerging: 92% of C-suite are actively cultivating an 'AI elite,' 60% plan layoffs for employees who refuse to adopt AI, and 67% believe their company has already suffered a data breach from unapproved AI tools — yet 36% lack any formal plan for supervising AI agents and 35% admit they could not immediately 'pull the plug' on a rogue agent. The survey identifies five critical failure modes blocking enterprise AI transformation: strategy without substance, the two-tiered workplace divide, a trust-and-resistance cycle, security and governance gaps, and a persistent disconnect between individual productivity gains and organizational ROI.

title: OpenAI Turns to Amazon as Microsoft Partnership 'Limited Our Ability' to Win Enterprise
summary: An internal OpenAI memo reveals the company is embracing its $50 billion Amazon partnership as a key enterprise growth driver while openly criticizing its Microsoft relationship and attacking rival Anthropic's revenue claims and compute strategy.
content: OpenAI revenue chief Denise Dresser sent a company-wide memo on April 13 touting the firm's new alliance with Amazon — which committed up to $50 billion in investment in late February — as critical for reaching enterprise customers, while explicitly stating that the Microsoft partnership 'has limited our ability to meet enterprises where they are,' particularly customers who build on Amazon Web Services' Bedrock platform. The memo comes as OpenAI battles Anthropic for enterprise dominance: Anthropic's Claude has become the leading enterprise AI platform, with industry observers at the HumanX conference in San Francisco calling it 'Claude mania' and describing adoption as 'a religion.' OpenAI was valued at over $850 billion in March while Anthropic stands at $380 billion, and both companies are racing toward IPOs. Dresser alleged that Anthropic's claimed $30 billion run-rate revenue is inflated by approximately $8 billion due to how it accounts for revenue-sharing with Amazon and Google — a claim Anthropic disputes, saying its accounting is consistent with GAAP. She also claimed Anthropic made a 'strategic misstep' by not acquiring enough compute, characterizing its strategy as built on 'fear, restriction, and the idea that a small group of elites should control AI' — a pointed contrast to OpenAI's positioning as it seeks to bring its enterprise revenue to parity with consumer revenue by year's end.
